flashcards.front: What is a tautology in propositional logic?
flashcards.back: A statement that is always true regardless of truth values of its propositional variables. Example: p ∨ ¬p

flashcards.front: State De Morgan's Law for negations

flashcards.back: Two statements are logically equivalent if they have identical truth values in all possible cases

flashcards.front: What is the contrapositive of p → q?
Name the equivalence: p ∨ (q ∧ r) ≡ (p ∨ q) ∧ (p ∨ r)

flashcards.back: A statement that is always false. Example: p ∧ ¬p
p ∧ ¬q (Using implication elimination and De Morgan's)
p ∨ (p ∧ q) ≡ p and p ∧ (p ∨ q) ≡ p

flashcards.front: How is exclusive OR (XOR) different from regular OR?
flashcards.back: p XOR q is true only when exactly one proposition is true
p ∨ F ≡ p and p ∧ T ≡ p
p ∨ T ≡ T and p ∧ F ≡ F
